topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. My thumb hovered over the 'delete' button, then retreated, paralyzed by a strange, hollow ache in my chest. It was from Sarah, the organizer, the one who had meticulously planned the entire evening—the decorations, the obscure playlist, the coordinated outfits. The subject line read simply: "Update Regarding the Celebration." Inside, the words were brief, polite, and devastatingly final: the surprise party was cancelled. I reread the message three times, the fluorescent light of my kitchen seeming to buzz with a harsh, irritating frequency. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the linoleum sounding offensively loud in the silence of the apartment. My hands began to tremble slightly, a fine vibration running up my forearms. I walked to the window, staring out at the street where I knew no one was gathering, the vibrant evening plans dissolving into a grey, indistinct smudge.

[story 2]

"No, no, this can't be right," I muttered, the words catching in my throat like dry leaves. I tapped the screen repeatedly, refreshing the message, hoping for a typo, a glitch in the system that would somehow restore the illusion. When the confirmation arrived, it was stark and unyielding. The entire event was scrubbed from the calendar. A cold knot tightened beneath my ribs, squeezing the air from my lungs. I dropped the phone onto the sofa cushion, the impact barely registering against the sudden, heavy weight settling over me. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room in tight, jerky circles. My jaw felt clenched so hard my molars ached. Every surface in the room suddenly seemed too bright, too sharp. I wanted to scream, but only a thin, reedy sound escaped my lips, a sound devoid of any real feeling.

[story 3]

I sat at the dining table, the remnants of the cake—now just a forgotten platter of frosting—looking mocking under the harsh overhead light. The notification had been unavoidable, a stark white block of text announcing the void where excitement had been scheduled. A slow, heavy sigh escaped me, a sound that seemed too deep for my own body. I picked up a wine glass, the crystal cool against my palm, and traced the rim with a fingertip. The silence in the house amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. My shoulders slumped forward, a profound sense of deflation washing over me, leaving me feeling strangely weighted down, as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold. I didn't move for a long time, just letting the stillness settle, the disappointment a thick, suffocating blanket around my awareness.
